children.name: Fulmarus
children.description: Fulmars — stocky, gull-like petrels of northern and southern cold waters. They resemble gulls but are true tubenoses, and their closest relatives are the giant petrels. Named from Old Norse 'fúllmár' meaning 'foul gull', referring to the oily vomit they eject at intruders.

children.name: Fulmarus glacialis
children.description: A common seabird of the North Atlantic and North Pacific. Grey above, white below, with a thick, tubenosed bill. Its name means 'foul gull' in Old Norse — a reference to the foul-smelling stomach oil it sprays at predators. Breeds on sea cliffs around the British Isles, Iceland, and Norway. Frequently seen in Swedish waters.

children.name: Fulmarus glacialoides
children.description: The Antarctic counterpart of the Northern Fulmar. Pale grey above, white below, with a bluish-grey bill. Breeds in large colonies on Antarctic coasts and islands. Feeds on krill, squid, and fish, often following ships.

children.name: Macronectes
children.description: Giant petrels — the largest members of the Procellariidae, with wingspans reaching 2 metres. They are aggressive scavengers and predators, often called 'stinkers' for their habit of feeding on carrion and the foul odour they emit. They breed on subantarctic islands.

children.name: Macronectes giganteus
children.description: A huge, aggressive petrel of Antarctica and the Southern Ocean. Dark brown or white morphs exist. Known for its powerful hooked bill and habit of killing other seabirds. Breeds on subantarctic islands and the Antarctic Peninsula. Classified as Least Concern but declining in parts of its range.

children.name: Puffinus
children.description: Shearwaters — medium-sized seabirds that fly low over the water with stiff, rapid wingbeats, 'shearing' the surface. They are among the most accomplished long-distance migrants. Puffinus was originally named for the Manx Shearwater's plump appearance; the puffin genus later took the name.

children.name: Puffinus puffinus
children.description: A classic shearwater of the North Atlantic. Black above, white below, with a distinctive 'shearing' flight — stiff wingbeats close to the water. Breeds in burrows on islands around the UK and Ireland, migrating to the South American coast for winter. Occasionally seen in Swedish waters during storms.

children.name: Puffinus mauretanicus
children.description: A critically endangered shearwater endemic to the Balearic Islands. Brownish-grey above, dirty white below. Post-breeding, it disperses into the Atlantic and Mediterranean, with regular sightings in the Bay of Biscay. Classified as Critically Endangered due to predation by introduced rats and cats.

children.name: Calonectris diomedea
children.description: A large shearwater of the Mediterranean. Brownish-grey above, white below, with a pale, yellowish bill. Breeds on Mediterranean islands, wintering off the West African coast. Regular but scarce in the Bay of Biscay and western approaches.

children.name: Ardenna gravis
children.description: A large, transatlantic shearwater that breeds on the Tristan da Cunha archipelago and Gough Island in the South Atlantic. After breeding, it undertakes a figure-of-eight migration up the eastern coast of North America, across the Atlantic to Europe, then down the African coast. Regularly seen from European seawatching points.

children.name: Pterodroma
children.description: Gadfly petrels — a diverse group of seabirds named for their darting, erratic flight (like a gadfly). They are among the most accomplished long-distance migrants, covering vast distances across oceans. Many species are confined to remote islands where they nest in burrows and return to land only under cover of darkness.

children.name: Pterodroma feae
children.description: A medium-sized gadfly petrel breeding on the Cape Verde Islands and Madeira (Bugio). Grey above, white below with a dark M-shaped pattern across the wings. Occasionally seen in European waters after storms. Named after Italian naturalist Leonardo Fea.

children.name: Pterodroma hasitata
children.description: Also known as the 'Cahow' — a critically endangered petrel once thought extinct. Breeds in the mountains of Hispaniola (Haiti and Dominican Republic). Striking black cap, white collar, and white rump. Classified as Endangered, with fewer than 1,000 breeding pairs.

children.name: Pachyptila
children.description: Prions — small, blue-grey petrels of the Southern Ocean, named for their saw-toothed bills that act as a filter for feeding on krill and other tiny crustaceans. They are among the most abundant seabirds on Earth, with Antarctic Prions numbering in the tens of millions.

children.name: Pachyptila desolata
children.description: The most abundant prion species, with an estimated 50 million individuals. Blue-grey above, white below, with a characteristic dark M across the wings. The bill has fine lamellae (comb-like plates) that filter krill from the water. Breeds in huge colonies on subantarctic islands.

children.name: Halobaena caerulea
children.description: A beautiful, small petrel with blue-grey upperparts and a white belly. Distinguished from prions by its black cap and white-tipped tail. Breeds on subantarctic islands including the Kerguelen and Crozet archipelagos.
